decision: Which OAuth provider type or set of providers should the system support for freelancer authentication?

grounding.existing_system: n/a — greenfield (C3): no existing system to conform to
grounding.note: arch-canon.json absent; option set reasoned from established architecture first principles; currency self-verified; all options are compliant with INV1 (OAuth delegation, no stored passwords); options scoped to single-server OAuth integration per INV6

options.summary: Application supports exactly one OAuth provider (Google); the sign-in flow hardcodes Google's authorization endpoint, token endpoint, and redirect URI; no provider-selection UI required.
options.reason_to_consider: Google OAuth is the most widely held provider account among freelancers; supporting one provider eliminates multi-provider routing complexity, simplifies the skeleton's authentication seam (FD2), and satisfies A2/R5/AC5 with the lowest integration surface area.

strengths: Single provider means a single set of redirect URIs, callback handlers, and token shapes to implement — minimises skeleton authentication seam complexity (FD2) under C2.
Google accounts are near-universally held by freelancers, directly satisfying R5 (account creation and authentication) for the expected user base.
AC5 explicitly names Google as a candidate provider ('e.g. Google or GitHub'); selecting it is a direct, lowest-risk path to meeting the acceptance criterion.
No provider-selection UI, no routing logic between providers, no multi-provider token storage differences — all removed from the implementation.
Google's OAuth 2.0 / OIDC library ecosystem is the most mature and best-documented across all candidate DP2 stacks.
costs: Freelancers without a Google account cannot authenticate; the system is functionally inaccessible to them.
Vendor dependency on Google's OAuth service availability and policy continuity; a Google account policy change (e.g., scope or consent screen change) directly impacts all users.
Harder to add a second provider later without refactoring the provider-specific code paths if the integration is built tightly around Google's endpoints.

options.summary: Application supports exactly one OAuth provider (GitHub); the sign-in flow targets GitHub's authorization and token endpoints; intended for a developer/freelancer audience.
options.reason_to_consider: AC5 explicitly names GitHub as a candidate provider; GitHub accounts are commonly held by technical freelancers (the likely primary user base for a time-tracking tool), and GitHub OAuth is equally simple to integrate as Google OAuth.

strengths: GitHub accounts are commonly held by technical freelancers — high coverage for the likely primary user demographic.
AC5 explicitly names GitHub as an acceptable provider; selecting it directly satisfies the acceptance criterion.
Single provider means the same low integration surface area as the Google option — one redirect URI, one callback handler, one token shape.
GitHub OAuth 2.0 libraries are mature across all candidate DP2 stacks (Passport GitHub strategy, OmniAuth GitHub, python-social-auth).
costs: Non-technical freelancers (designers, writers, consultants) may not hold GitHub accounts, limiting accessibility for a broader freelancer audience.
GitHub accounts are less universally held than Google accounts for a general-freelancer user base — potentially narrower user coverage than Google.
Vendor dependency on GitHub/Microsoft's OAuth service availability and API policy.
Same provider lock-in characteristic as Google single-provider: adding a second provider later requires refactoring if built tightly around GitHub's endpoints.

options.summary: Application integrates with a provider-agnostic OAuth 2.0/OIDC layer (e.g., Passport.js multi-strategy, OmniAuth, Authlib); which provider(s) are active is configurable via environment variables; the application itself is provider-neutral.
options.reason_to_consider: A provider-agnostic abstraction allows the application to support Google, GitHub, or any OIDC-compatible provider without code changes — only configuration changes; this satisfies A6 (no lock-in to a single vendor dependency) and gives the operator flexibility to switch or add providers.

strengths: Provider-neutral design: switching or adding providers requires only configuration changes, not code changes — aligns with A6's vendor-agnosticism principle.
A single OIDC abstraction layer can handle token normalization across providers, reducing per-provider divergence in the auth code path.
Lower risk of provider lock-in over the application's lifetime; a provider policy change forces only a configuration swap, not a code change.
Multi-strategy auth libraries (Passport.js, OmniAuth, Authlib) are mature and well-supported across all candidate DP2 stacks.
costs: Higher initial implementation complexity than a single-provider approach: the multi-provider routing logic, provider-selection UI, and per-provider configuration management must all be built and tested under C2.
The aPRD names a personal-tool-scale single-freelancer application (A13, A7); configuring multiple providers for one user is operationally complex for minimal benefit.
More application state to manage: the freelancer's provider identity must record which provider was used, in case multiple providers are active.
Each additional provider registered on a given OAuth platform (Google Cloud Console, GitHub OAuth Apps) requires separate client ID/secret pairs to manage as secrets.

no_pick_note: Live, unranked alternatives. EVALUATE-DECIDE scores against CONSTRAINTS / ACCEPTANCE / NFRs and picks (role 4); OPTION-GEN does not recommend.
